content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers 21 (NUM_021)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

This log records all verses in Numbers Chapter 21 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) produced meaningfully different renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 21:2 -- "my hand" vs. "our hand" (person of the pronoun in the vow)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If giving you will give this people into my hand, and I will devote to destruction their cities" | Preserves MT singular be-yadi and singular ve-hakharamti |
| LOGOS | "If you will surely give this people into my hand, then I will utterly devote their cities to destruction" | Preserves MT singular; infinitive absolute rendered with doubling |
| PNEUMA | "If you will surely give this people into our hand, then we will utterly destroy their cities" | Shifts to plural for consistency with collective speaker; renders cherem as "destroy" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "our hand...we will utterly devote...to destruction." The plural resolves the English awkwardness of a singular pronoun for a collective speaker. PNEUMA's instinct on the pronoun is followed, but "utterly devote...to destruction" (LOGOS's phrasing) is retained over PNEUMA's simpler "utterly destroy" because it preserves the sacral-destructive force of the cherem terminology. The infinitive absolute is rendered with doubling per binding convention ("surely give...utterly devote").

---

## 21:4 -- "Sea of Reeds" vs. "Red Sea" (rendering of Yam Suf)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Sea of Reeds" (literal) | suf = "reed"; preserves Hebrew meaning |
| LOGOS | "the Red Sea" (conventional) | Follows traditional English rendering and LXX thalassan eruthran |
| PNEUMA | "the Sea of Reeds" (literal) | Preserves the Hebrew suf against conventional usage |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the Sea of Reeds" with EMET and PNEUMA. The Hebrew word suf means "reed," not "red." The conventional rendering "Red Sea" derives from the LXX's eruthran thalassan, which may reflect a different geographical identification. Fidelity to the MT requires "Sea of Reeds."

---

## 21:4 -- "the soul of the people grew short" vs. "became impatient" vs. "the spirit of the people grew short" (rendering of va-tiqtsar nefesh ha-am)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the soul of the people grew short on the way" | Literal rendering of qatsar nefesh idiom |
| LOGOS | "the soul of the people became impatient on the way" | Interprets the idiom for English clarity |
| PNEUMA | "the spirit of the people grew short on the journey" | Renders nefesh as "spirit" rather than "soul"; preserves the idiom's imagery |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the soul of the people grew short on the way" with EMET. The idiom qatsar nefesh ("shortness of soul") is vivid and comprehensible in English -- the soul constricted, patience exhausted. LOGOS's "became impatient" decodes the idiom but loses the physicality. PNEUMA's "spirit" is defensible but "soul" is the standard SOPHIA rendering of nefesh established in earlier books.

---

## 21:5 -- "our soul loathes" vs. "our throats loathe" (rendering of nafshenu qatsah)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "our soul loathes the contemptible bread" | nefesh = "soul"; qeloqel = "contemptible" |
| LOGOS | "our soul loathes this worthless food" | nefesh = "soul"; ha-lechem ha-qeloqel = "this worthless food" |
| PNEUMA | "our throats loathe this worthless food" | nefesh = "throats" (physical sense); qeloqel = "worthless" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "our soul loathes this worthless food" -- combining EMET/LOGOS's "soul" with LOGOS/PNEUMA's "worthless food." PNEUMA's "throats" captures the physical sense of nefesh (throat, appetite), but "soul" is the established SOPHIA rendering. "Worthless" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is preferred over "contemptible" (EMET) as it better renders the polpel qeloqel, which describes the food as trifling and insufficient rather than morally despicable. "Food" rather than "bread" is adopted because lechem in this context refers to the manna as sustenance generally.

---

## 21:5 -- "contemptible" vs. "worthless" (rendering of ha-qeloqel)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the contemptible bread" | Emphasizes the disdainful, scornful tone |
| LOGOS | "this worthless food" | Emphasizes the perceived lack of value |
| PNEUMA | "this worthless food" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "worthless" with LOGOS and PNEUMA (two-to-one). The Hebrew qeloqel (from qalal, "to be light/trifling") denotes something of negligible worth. "Contemptible" (EMET) adds a moral dimension not primary in the root. "Worthless" captures the people's complaint that the manna is insubstantial and unsatisfying.

---

## 21:11 -- "before Moab" vs. "faces Moab" (rendering of al penei Mo'av)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "upon the face of Moab" | Extremely literal; preserves panah/panim idiom |
| LOGOS | "that is before Moab" | Standard English equivalent for al penei |
| PNEUMA | "that faces Moab toward the rising of the sun" | Dynamic: makes the wilderness the grammatical subject "facing" Moab |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "that is before Moab" with LOGOS. "Before" accurately renders al penei in its spatial sense. EMET's "upon the face of" is overly literal for a common prepositional phrase. PNEUMA's "faces" is acceptable but personifies the wilderness unnecessarily.

---

## 21:12 -- "Valley of Zered" vs. "Wadi Zered" (rendering of Nakhal Zered)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Valley of Zered" | Standard rendering of nakhal |
| LOGOS | "the Valley of Zered" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "the Wadi Zered" | Uses the technical geographical term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the Wadi Zered" with PNEUMA. In the poetic citation of vv. 14-15, "wadis" is also used for nekhalim. Consistency requires using the same English term for the same Hebrew word throughout the chapter. "Wadi" precisely describes a seasonal desert watercourse and has been naturalized in English.

---

## 21:14-15 -- "valleys" vs. "wadis" (rendering of nekhalim in the Book of the Wars citation)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the valleys of Arnon...the slope of the valleys" | Standard "valleys" for nekhalim |
| LOGOS | "the valleys of the Arnon...the slope of the valleys" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "the wadis of the Arnon...the slope of the wadis" | Technical geographical term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "wadis" with PNEUMA for the reasons stated in v. 12 above. The Arnon system consists of deeply incised seasonal watercourses, not broad valleys. "Wadis" is the precise term.

---

## 21:15 -- "dwelling" vs. "settlement" (rendering of le-shevet Ar)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the dwelling of Ar" | Literal: shevet as "sitting/dwelling" |
| LOGOS | "the dwelling of Ar" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "the settlement of Ar" | Interprets shevet as referring to a settled community |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the dwelling of Ar" with EMET and LOGOS (two-to-one). The Hebrew shevet (from yashav, "to sit/dwell") is broad enough to encompass a settlement, but "dwelling" is closer to the root and avoids implying a specific urban form.

---

## 21:18 -- "delved" vs. "excavated" vs. "hollowed out" (rendering of karah in the Well Song)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "excavated it" | Direct translation of karah |
| LOGOS | "delved" | Archaic English synonym preserving poetic register |
| PNEUMA | "hollowed out" | Dynamic: conveys the physical action vividly |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "excavated" with EMET. "Delved" (LOGOS) is archaic and may confuse modern readers. "Hollowed out" (PNEUMA) is vivid but implies shaping a cavity rather than digging downward. "Excavated" is precise, concrete, and accessible.

---

## 21:20 -- "field of Moab" vs. "country of Moab" (rendering of sdeh Mo'av)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the field of Moab" | Literal rendering of sadeh |
| LOGOS | "the field of Moab" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "the country of Moab" | Interprets sadeh as broader territory |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the field of Moab" with EMET and LOGOS (two-to-one). The Hebrew sadeh means "field, open land" -- it denotes the open plateau of Moab, not the country as a whole. "Country" overgeneralizes.

---

## 21:20 -- "the Jeshimon" vs. "the wasteland" (rendering of ha-Yeshimon)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Jeshimon" | Preserves as proper noun |
| LOGOS | "the face of the Jeshimon" | Preserves as proper noun with literal al penei |
| PNEUMA | "the wasteland" | Translates the meaning (from yasham, "to be desolate") |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the face of the Jeshimon" -- retaining al penei here (unlike v. 11) because the phrase "overlooks the face of" conveys the panoramic vista from Pisgah's summit, and "the Jeshimon" functions as a recognized geographical name for the barren terrain toward the Dead Sea. PNEUMA's "wasteland" loses the specificity.

---

## 21:22 -- "your border" vs. "your territory" (rendering of gevulekha)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "your border" | Literal rendering of gevul |
| LOGOS | "your border" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "your territory" | Interprets gevul as the enclosed territory, not just the boundary line |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "your border" with EMET and LOGOS (two-to-one). The Hebrew gevul means "border, boundary" -- the request is to pass through until they have crossed the boundary, not traversed the entire territory. The spatial logic of the passage requires "border."

---

## 21:24 -- "possessed" vs. "took possession of" (rendering of va-yirash)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "possessed his land" | Bare verb form |
| LOGOS | "possessed his land" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "took possession of his land" | More active phrasing |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "took possession of his land" with PNEUMA. The hiphil of yarash carries the active sense of seizing and dispossessing; "took possession" better conveys this than the stative "possessed."

---

## 21:24 -- "strong" vs. "fortified" (rendering of az describing the Ammonite border)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "strong was the border of the Sons of Ammon" | Literal rendering of az |
| LOGOS | "the border of the Sons of Ammon was strong" | Same adjective, reordered for English |
| PNEUMA | "the border of the Sons of Ammon was fortified" | Interprets az in military context |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "fortified" with PNEUMA. The context is military -- explaining why Israel's conquest halted at Ammon's border. "Fortified" captures the functional sense: the border was defensively hardened, not merely "strong" in an abstract sense. The LXX reads "Jazer" here (a different parsing entirely), but the MT reading az is followed.

---

## 21:25 -- "villages" vs. "surrounding towns" vs. "her daughters" (rendering of benoteiha)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "her daughters" | Preserves the Hebrew mother-city/daughter-village metaphor |
| LOGOS | "all its villages" | Decodes the idiom for English readers |
| PNEUMA | "all its surrounding towns" | More descriptive decoding of the idiom |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "its villages" with LOGOS. "Her daughters" (EMET) preserves the metaphor but confuses English readers unfamiliar with the idiom. "Surrounding towns" (PNEUMA) adds the qualifier "surrounding" that is not in the Hebrew. "Villages" strikes the right balance.

---

## 21:27 -- "the ones who speak in proverbs" vs. "the ballad-singers" (rendering of ha-moshelim)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the ones speaking proverbs" | From mashal, "proverb, parable" |
| LOGOS | "the ones who speak in proverbs" | Same approach, smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "the ballad-singers" | Interprets the function as musical performance |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the ones who speak in proverbs" with EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew mashal means "proverb, parable, taunt-song" -- the root identifies the genre of speech, not the medium of delivery. "Ballad-singers" (PNEUMA) imports a musical performance context not explicit in the text.

---

## 21:29 -- "You are undone" vs. "You are destroyed" (rendering of avadta)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You are destroyed" | Direct rendering of avad |
| LOGOS | "You are undone" | Captures the broader sense of ruin and dissolution |
| PNEUMA | "You are destroyed" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "You are undone" with LOGOS. Within the taunt-song's poetic register, "undone" carries richer connotations -- dissolution, unraveling, loss of national coherence -- than the blunter "destroyed." The Hebrew avad encompasses perishing, being lost, and being ruined, not merely physical destruction.

---

## 21:29 -- "He has given" vs. "He made" (rendering of natan in the Chemosh stanza)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "He has given his sons as fugitives" | Literal natan = "give" |
| LOGOS | "He has given his sons as fugitives" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "He made his sons fugitives" | Renders natan dynamically as "made" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He has given his sons as fugitives" with EMET and LOGOS (two-to-one). The verb natan ("to give") is theologically loaded here: Chemosh is accused of surrendering his own worshippers. "Given...as" preserves this sense of divine abandonment. "Made" (PNEUMA) reduces the theological force to mere causation.

---

## 21:32 -- "villages" vs. "surrounding towns" vs. "her daughters" (rendering of benoteiha for Jazer)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "her daughters" | Same Hebrew idiom as v. 25 |
| LOGOS | "its villages" | Consistent with v. 25 rendering |
| PNEUMA | "its surrounding towns" | Consistent with PNEUMA's v. 25 rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "its villages" with LOGOS for consistency with v. 25.

---

## 21:33 -- "went out against them" vs. "went out to meet them" (rendering of va-yetse liqratam)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "went out to meet them" | Literal liqrat = "to meet, encounter" |
| LOGOS | "went out against them" | Interprets the hostile intent of the meeting |
| PNEUMA | "went out to meet them" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "went out against them" with LOGOS. While liqrat literally means "to meet/encounter," the context makes the hostile intent clear (the next phrase specifies "to battle at Edrei"). "Against them" captures the military aggression more directly and avoids the neutral connotation of "to meet."

---

## 21:34 -- "who was dwelling" vs. "who dwelt" vs. "who lived" (rendering of yoshev)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who was dwelling in Heshbon" | Preserves participial force of yoshev |
| LOGOS | "who was dwelling in Heshbon" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "who lived in Heshbon" | Simple past tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who dwelt in Heshbon." Sihon has been defeated, so the past tense is appropriate. "Dwelt" is more concise than the periphrastic "was dwelling" (EMET/LOGOS) and more formal than "lived" (PNEUMA), fitting the register of divine speech.

---

## 21:35 -- "struck him" vs. "struck him down" (addition of "down")

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they struck him and his sons and all his people" | No addition |
| LOGOS | "they struck him and his sons and all his people" | No addition |
| PNEUMA | "they struck him down, and his sons, and all his people" | Adds "down" for finality |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they struck him" without "down," following EMET and LOGOS (two-to-one). The Hebrew va-yakku does not include a directional particle. The totality of the defeat is expressed by the subsequent clause ("until there was no survivor remaining to him"), making "down" redundant.

---

## 21:35 -- "possessed his land" vs. "took possession of his land" (rendering of va-yirshu)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they possessed his land" | Stative rendering |
| LOGOS | "they possessed his land" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "they took possession of his land" | Active rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they took possession of his land" with PNEUMA, for the same reason as v. 24: the hiphil of yarash carries active, acquisitive force.

---

*In council, the three translators showed strong agreement on the prose narrative of Numbers 21. The greatest divergences arose in the rendering of the three poetic fragments (vv. 14-15, 17-18, 27-30), in the lexical choice for geographical terms (wadi vs. valley, Jeshimon vs. wasteland), and in the degree to which Hebrew idioms should be decoded for the English reader (benoteiha as "daughters" vs. "villages," ha-moshelim as "proverb-speakers" vs. "ballad-singers"). The manuscript images from the Leningrad Codex confirm all readings; the Vaticanus LXX was consulted for the significant divergences noted by all three translators (v. 6: "death-dealing" vs. "fiery" serpents; v. 24: "Jazer" vs. "strong"; v. 30: substantially different text), but in each case the MT was followed as the primary source.*

-- SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter
